figures.caption: Figure 6. From left to right: visualizations of µ̂E(x), µ̂ (o)(x), and Pxµ̂E(x), where Px is the projection operator onto the tangent space TxM . The ground-truth drift vector is superimposed as blue arrows. The associated local patch mapped via U3 and rotation is overlaid to enhance the visualization.

Table 2. Summary of various evaluation metrics. µ̂(x) is the estimator of µ(o)(x), which can be µ̂E , µ̂ (o), or Pxµ̂E , where Px is the projection to TxM , listed in the top. π̂(x) is the estimator of π(o)(x), which is π̂(o)(x). Θ(µ(o), µ̂) is the angle between µ(o) and µ̂ with the unit radian. ∥ sinΘ∥F is the subspace distance between the dominant 2D eigenspaces of π̂(o) and π(o).

Table 1. Summary of various evaluation metrics. µ̂(x) is the estimator of µ(o)(x), which can be µ̂E , µ̂ (o), or Pxµ̂E , where Px is the projection to TxM , listed in the top. π̂(x) is the estimator of π(o)(x), which is π̂(o)(x). Θ(µ̂, µ(o)) is the angle between µ(o) and µ̂ with the unit radian. ∥ sinΘ∥F is the subspace distance between the dominant 2D eigenspaces of π̂(o) and π(o).
Figure 3. Histograms of drift estimation errors over 1000 independent SDE simulations, comparing µ̂E and µ̂(o) to the true drift vector field µ(o) at (0, 0, 1)⊤.

Figure 4. Histograms of the errors of the estimated diffusion matrix u⊤i π̂ (o)uj over 1000 independent SDE simulations (labeled as (i, j) in the subplots) compared to the true vector fields u⊤i π (o)uj , for ellipsoids of varying eccentricities.

Figure 2. From left to right: visualizations of µ̂E(x), µ̂ (o)(x), and Pxµ̂E(x), where Px is the projection operator onto the tangent space TxM , for base-point samples x drawn uniformly from a spherical cap centered at (1, 0, 0)⊤ and observed on an ellipsoid with eccentricity (2, 1.5, 1), shown from two viewing angles. The ground-truth drift vector is superimposed as blue arrows.

Figure 5. Using the occupation density for trajectory length n = 108 and ∆ = 10−2 as an accurate estimate for the invariant density ϕX , we compare the invariant density to the estimate L̂(o) based on the first n data-points of the trajectory to measure the convergence rate of the empirical density.

Figure 1. Using the occupation density for trajectory length n = 108, time-step ∆ = 10−2, and physical time T = 106 as an accurate estimate for the invariant density ϕX , we compare the invariant density to the estimate L̂(o) based on the first n data-points of the trajectory on each observed ellipsoid to measure the convergence rate of the empirical density.
